chapters.title: A message from Olga Huckins
chapters.text:
In January 1958, Olga Owens Huckins, a friend of Rachel Carson, wrote her a letter. Huckins was living in Massachusetts, where the state government was working hard to control the mosquito population. Their main tool was a mix of DDT, a pesticide, and fuel oil. The plan called for spraying certain areas with this mix, and Huckins' home was in the spray zone.

At the time, DDT makers and government officials told the public the pesticide was safe, and many campaigns pushed that message. But the morning after Huckins' neighborhood was sprayed, she found several of her favorite birds lying dead in her yard. The spraying had not wiped out the mosquitoes. In the long run, it had made the problem worse.

When Carson began speaking out to rally the public against DDT and other harmful pesticides, pushback from the chemical industry was expected. Carson was not shaken. The message of Silent Spring reached President Kennedy, who set up a board of inquiry. The President's Panel later reported back and confirmed that Carson's claims about the harm done by chemical pesticides were sound.

It takes a single individual to start a revolution.

Carson brought the science of ecology to the average American's door, and people began to grasp the danger. Pressure from the public pushed government at all levels to look at environmental laws.

This summary will make you aware of a quiet kind of poisoning, the kind that happens when chemicals are spread across the land. You will also see how human activity is putting the balance of nature under strain. Read it through to learn how you can help bring back that balance.

chapters.title: The abstractions of life make us humble to death
chapters.text:
The whole story of life on Earth has always involved the way living things interact with their environment. The plants and animals alive today have been shaped by the world they live in. Given the age of the Earth and the activities of the past few centuries, a new normal has set in. One species, humans, has built up enough intelligence and power to flip the old order. Nature is now at the mercy of human activity, and the effects are sharper than ever.

The threat to people and to nature at large is starting to come into focus.

Droughts, tsunamis, floods, landslides, and climate change are all signs that the Earth is under stress. Some experts see these problems but do not think through what they mean in the bigger picture. Business, production, and trade now run on a massive industrial scale. The goal is often to make another dollar, whatever the cost to nature. Few people worry about the heat given off by bitcoin mining server farms, as long as the money keeps coming.

It is time for the human race to step up, push aside the false promises, and stop accepting the claims of the clever and the unverified. The public of the twentieth century paid the price when insect control efforts failed. The job now belongs to ordinary people, armed with the truth about what our inventions really do, to decide what to accept and what to reject.

Jean Rostand once said that the duty to endure gives us the right to know.

Every living person is exposed to dangerous chemicals, from the first breath to the last. That exposure is the result of a sudden boom in industries making synthetic chemicals that kill insects.

chapters.title: Understanding the interconnectedness of the earth's waters, soil, animals, and greenery
chapters.text:
The danger of widespread contamination in the water people use is not only the toxicity but the cancer risk. Authorities can barely control the leak of some of these harmful substances into public water supplies. Dr. W. C. Hueper of the National Cancer Institute warned that the cancer risk from drinking polluted water would rise sharply in the years to come, and that future is already here.

Water pollution is a complex issue and a part of the environmental strain caused by industry.

The soil is the next victim of human contamination. The thin crust of soil that covers the land over Earth's waters and continents shapes the life of every land animal. Without this soil, plants and animals cannot thrive, and the loss of plants brings harm to animals, directly or indirectly.

With the steady use of pesticides, hard-to-break-down residues pile up in the soil around the world. Some of these substances are broken down into less harmful forms by natural processes in the soil, but the buildup is faster than the breakdown. In 1960, experts met at Syracuse University to study soil health. They warned that if humans misuse strong tools like chemicals and radiation, the soil would be harmed, and arthropods would take over.

The plant life on the planet is part of a living network that shows the close and crucial partnership between the Earth and its plants. Sometimes that partnership has to be disturbed, but honesty demands that we think carefully about what our actions mean in time and place.

chapters.title: Humans are self-hypnotic; they wage wars against nature and scar the planet Earth
chapters.text:
There is a real hurdle for anyone who tries to judge the loss of wildlife from chemical pesticides. Experts say the losses have been severe, and in some places catastrophic. Regulatory bodies, on the other hand, deny or downplay the harm, calling it small or unimportant.

Most pesticides are not selective. They cannot be aimed at one species. They are used because they are deadly. They poison every form of life they touch, from a pet cat to a rabbit in the field, from farm cattle to the horned lark in the sky.

In 1954, the year light spraying of pesticides began in the U.S., things at first looked normal. The next spring, Professor George Wallace, an ornithologist at Michigan State University, and one of his graduate students watched the yearly return of robins to the campus. The birds settled back into their usual spots without knowing what had changed. Soon, red flags appeared. Dead and dying robins turned up all over the campus. Their normal feeding habits shifted, and so did their roosting patterns. Far fewer nests were found than the year before, and very few young robins appeared. The pattern kept repeating every spring after that.

Birds have been shown by many studies to play a key role in keeping insects in check.

For every robin that came back to the MSU campus, the sprayed area acted like a deadly trap and killed the bird within a week.

Fisheries are a vital resource that affects the wellbeing of many people. Aquatic animals cannot walk away from their habitat, and that habitat is slowly becoming the cause of their decline. The serious threat from chemicals getting into waterways can no longer be ignored.

If we put even a small part of the money we spend on stronger pesticides into useful research, we would be in a much better position to use safer materials and keep poisons out of the ecosystem.

chapters.title: Aiming at the duck and killing the child
chapters.text:
The market for toxic and poisonous substances is so well established that anyone with money can walk into a store and buy one. A short look at the shelves in any average supermarket shows how many products with harmful base ingredients are up for sale. Some of these substances are more dangerous than hard drugs.

For example, many moth-proofing products have labels in tiny print warning that the can is under pressure and can explode near heat or flame. A compound often found in homes, especially kitchens, is chlordane. Even though a top official at the Food and Drug Administration pointed out the serious risk of living in a chlordane-sprayed house, these products are still on the market. Some household items contain even more harmful compounds, such as dieldrin.

The hazards of gardening with chemical products or using cleaning sprays at home are also part of the picture. The warning labels are so small that they are easy to miss. Herbicides are sold under brand names that say nothing about their ingredients or what they actually do. Figuring out if a product contains chlordane or dieldrin takes careful reading of the fine print, often hidden on the least visible side of the package.

Each repeated exposure to a hazardous chemical adds to the body's total toxic load.

The packaging for some risky products sold in garden or hardware stores rarely mentions the dangers. The pictures on the box show a happy family scene, like a mother gardening with her daughter while they apply the chemicals to vegetables, windowsills, or even the living room. In some ads, small children are shown tumbling on the grass with a dog.

For context, body fat, which makes up about 18 percent of body weight, has many important functions that can be disrupted by the buildup of drugs and chemicals.

chapters.title: The microscopic view and nature's affront
chapters.text:
The way natural chemicals work inside cells, when properly understood, is one of the wonders of the living world. All the parts involved in these processes are tiny, which makes the whole system feel like a grand miracle.

We cannot ignore what it means to flood the environment with chemicals that can damage key parts of our cells, like chromosomes. Since chromosomes shape the identity of a cell and the organism it belongs to, breaking them is a heavy price to pay for saving potatoes or wiping out mosquitoes in the yard. With a real commitment to protecting our genetic heritage, we can lower this risk if we choose to.

Our genes are a temporary loan from our ancestors, meant to be passed on to the next generation.

In 1775, Sir Percivall Pott, a British surgeon, noticed a possible link between the soot-covered bodies of chimney sweeps and the high rate of scrotal cancer among them. He could not prove it at the time, but later research confirmed the harmful chemical in soot, supporting Pott's theory.

Here is how the Pott discovery applies to us. Pesticides used to control nature can cause cancer. Animal tests show that five or six of the most common pesticides are carcinogenic. Around the same period, when modern and improved pesticides came into wide use, the rate of leukemia began to climb steadily.

Nature has refused to sit still. Studies done after the heavy spraying of the twentieth century show a large jump in the population of the very insects the spraying was meant to control. In Ontario, for example, the number of blackflies grew seventeen times after spraying. In another case, England had a huge outbreak of the cabbage aphid, a scale of attack with no equal in history, after organic phosphorus chemicals were sprayed.

Nature adapts and fights back.

chapters.title: The necessity of charting a new course — the sign
chapters.text:
If Charles Darwin were alive today, he would have been amazed and delighted by the insects and other living things around the world. His excitement would come from how clearly his ideas about the survival of the fittest were being proven. Under the sudden shock of heavy chemical spraying, the weaker members of a species die off first, since they cannot adapt quickly.

In many parts of the world, only the toughest and most resilient members of each species are still here, in spite of our efforts to control them.

Dr. Briejèr, who headed the Plant Protection Service in Holland, argued that accepting a little damage now is wiser than putting it off and suffering a much larger loss later. The smart path is to keep chemical spraying to the barest minimum, rather than going all out with the products on hand. Dr. Briejèr's point is that pressure on the pest population should be kept as light as possible.

The deck of cards sits on the table, and it is up to us to decide how to play them. Life on Earth has put up with a great deal of chemical pollution, but humans now know better. We are aware of the frightening risks of our destructive activity. We can no longer accept the advice of those who want to soak our fragile ecosystem in poisonous chemicals. We have to weigh the tradeoffs and look at other practical options.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
During her time working for the government, Rachel Carson and her scientific colleagues could not stay quiet after they saw the alarming biological effects of widespread DDT use and other long-lasting poisons in so-called agricultural control programs.

Soon after the war, when the dangers were already well known, Carson tried to get magazines to publish pieces that would educate the public. Progress was slow, and the damage done by toxic pesticides and herbicides to wildlife and humans kept piling up. She took action, and her work paid off. Even though the companies making huge profits from these products attacked her reputation and rejected most of her work, the public ended up better informed about the problem.

The Earth's ecosystem faces many challenges, including the need for living things to share space and resources. Our decisions affect many lives, especially as the human population grows. We have to guide these factors carefully, in our favor. Until we do, we will not make peace with the insects around us.

Try this. The state of the Earth today is the result of the actions of people who lived before you. What will you do to leave a habitable planet for your children? If you are not sure where to start, first look at the parts of your life that add to environmental damage. Then decide which of those you can fix. Finally, take real steps to make up for the harm you can correct.
